title: Re-edging a chipboard kitchen unit door
subtitle: Repair or replace the iron-on edge banding on a kitchen cupboard door
excerpt: How to repair or replace iron-on edge banding on a chipboard kitchen door or shelf: removing the old banding, cleaning the substrate, ironing on new pre-glued banding, trimming the overhang, and finishing the corners.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-glued strip of paper, melamine, or real-wood veneer that bonds to the raw edge of a chipboard or MDF panel when heat is applied. The strip is slightly wider than the panel thickness; the overhang is trimmed after bonding with a sharp blade or a purpose-made edge trimmer. Available in melamine colours to match standard kitchen carcass boards.

The particleboard substrate under the melamine surface of a kitchen unit door or carcass panel. Chipboard absorbs moisture readily if the edge banding lifts, causing the core to swell and the banding to detach further. Re-edging promptly when banding lifts at a corner prevents progressive swelling.

content.text: to moisture. Replacing the banding is a 15-minute job using a domestic iron set to the cotton or high setting; no specialist tools are needed.
Edge banding has lifted at a corner or along a full edge.
Banding is missing entirely from a damaged or cut edge.
A cut shelf or panel needs its exposed edge finished to match the rest of the unit.

Heat the existing banding with a household iron held against it for 10 to 15 seconds to soften the adhesive; slide the filling knife under the edge and prise it away while still warm. If the banding comes away in sections, work in short heated sections rather than the full length at once.
Step 2, clean and prepare the edge.

Scrape off any remaining adhesive residue from the chipboard edge with the utility knife blade held flat. Sand the edge lightly with the sanding block to produce a flat, dust-free surface. The banding adhesive needs a clean, flat substrate to bond to; old adhesive lumps will prevent the new banding from lying flat.

Cut a length of pre-glued edge banding 30 mm longer than the edge to be covered. Position the strip centrally over the edge with the glue side against the chipboard. Run the iron along the strip at a steady walking pace, pressing firmly. The adhesive activates in 8 to 10 seconds per section; move too fast and the bond will be weak, too slow and you will scorch the facing. Immediately press a dry cloth firmly along the freshly bonded section while the adhesive is still warm.

Once the banding has cooled (two to three minutes), trim the overhang on both long edges with the utility knife. Hold the blade at a shallow angle against the panel face and shave the overhang off in a single stroke. Sand the trimmed edge lightly with 180-grit on the sanding block to remove the knife mark and blend the banding edge flush with the panel face. Trim the short ends at the corners with a square cut using the knife and a straight edge.
The iron was not hot enough or did not dwell long enough on that section. Re-heat with the iron and press immediately with a cloth until the bond forms.

Edge banding is sold in standard kitchen board colours (white, beech, oak, grey). Compare a sample of the banding to the door face in natural light before cutting. Slight sheen differences between the banding and the door face are normal and less noticeable once the door is fitted back in position.
